Patch Set 3: Verified+1

Passed enforced test review-ldiskfs-dne on RHEL 8.10 / x86_64 uploaded by Onyx Autotest from onyx-153vm7: https://testing.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/ecf17ab1-7174-4902-add5-0cd0be57c1a4 ran 5 tests.

Patch Set 3:

In addition to the warning, these requirements should also be documented in the lustre/doc/lctl-nodemap-add-offset.8 and lctl-nodemap-modify.8 and lctl-nodemap-add-idmap.8 man pages, so that users can know more details of the security implications of why these checks are in place instead of just a one-line warning message.

I'm wondering whether these checks should actually generate errors, or if there _might_ be some reasonable cases that these are useful?  We want to allow flexibility for unexpected use cases, but we shouldn't make it easy for users to shoot themselves in the foot with bad security choices (e.g. squash IDs to be in some other Tenant's ID space).
Uploaded patch set 4.

(1 comment)

Patch Set 4:

Patch is updated to add comments about offset in the relevant man pages.

I do not think we should directly return an error if we detect inconsistencies between offset and other nodemap parameters such as squash values or explicit mappings. There might be some corner cases we have not thought about yet and that would legitimate what could be considered a misconfiguration otherwise.

Patch Set 4:

(12 comments)

Thanks, Sébastien! Just a few comments that I think would improve this patch, and some questions.

Patch Set 4:

Another point I am wondering about: At some point, we talked about whether such kind of checks should be done in kernel or user space. IIRC, one argument was that if commands would be allowed to be called from the ll_* api at some point, sanity checks in obd.c will no longer get hit, and therefore they should primarily be done on the kernel side when it comes to nodemaps. 

"lctl nodemap_activate" is another example where the format checking ('1', 'on', etc) only takes place in the nodemap_handler.c.

Should these cases therefore also be checked on the kernel side?

Patch Set 4:

(11 comments)
Uploaded patch set 5.

Patch Set 5:

Thanks Marc, patchset 5 addresses your remarks.

Patch Set 5:

> Patch Set 4:
> 
> Another point I am wondering about: At some point, we talked about whether such kind of checks should be done in kernel or user space. IIRC, one argument was that if commands would be allowed to be called from the ll_* api at some point, sanity checks in obd.c will no longer get hit, and therefore they should primarily be done on the kernel side when it comes to nodemaps. 
> 
> "lctl nodemap_activate" is another example where the format checking ('1', 'on', etc) only takes place in the nodemap_handler.c.
> 
> Should these cases therefore also be checked on the kernel side?

I considered this, and the patch would have been much simpler (no need to parse yaml...). But in this case it would be impossible to return useful warning messages to the user.

parents.subject: LU-18814 tests: run extra prog, execute NLOOPS mpi workloads

commit.subject: LU-18863 nodemap: warning for inconsistencies with offset
commit.message:
LU-18863 nodemap: warning for inconsistencies with offset

When offsets are defined on a nodemap, other properties related to id
mapping are impacted.
All squash values should be in the offset range [ 0, offset_limit-1 ],
otherwise squashed ids would escape offsetting.
Any explicit id mapping should map to an fsid in the offset range,
otherwise the mapped id would escape offsetting.
And the offset limit itself should be defined so that nobody (65534)
is included in the offset range.
lctl nodemap_add_offset, nodemap_modify and nodemap_add_idmap commands
are modified to print a warning when such an inconsistency is
detected.
lctl-nodemap-add-idmap.8, lctl-nodemap-add-offset.8 and
lctl-nodemap-modify.8 manpages are updated to explain these
constraints.

Test-Parameters: trivial testlist=sanity-sec
Signed-off-by: Sebastien Buisson <sbuisson@ddn.com>
